title: Hormuz Nears Standstill, Qatar Halts LNG Revival, and Russia Bans Diesel Exports
subhead: Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic collapsed to near-zero after Iran attacked a Qatari LNG carrier, Qatar paused production at the world's largest LNG complex, Russia banned diesel exports under Ukrainian drone pressure, and Gulf producers launched a price war for Asian market share — the energy fallout from the collapsing Iran ceasefire is compounding fast.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near-Zero as Qatar LNG Carrier Attacked
sections.body:
Tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz fell to a near standstill on Thursday, with only 21 commodity vessels transiting in either direction on Wednesday — one of the thinnest traffic days since the interim peace deal in mid-June, according to Kpler data reported by Bloomberg and the Hindu Business Line. That compares with an average of 34 daily transits during the three weeks after the deal, and a peak of 59 on June 24. During the war's worst phase, daily transits dropped below 20.

The collapse in traffic followed Iran's attacks on three commercial vessels near the strait earlier in the week, including Qatar's Al Rekayyat LNG tanker, which was disabled and abandoned by its crew. A US-sanctioned supertanker and an Iranian-flagged container ship were the only larger vessels observed transiting on Thursday. Some tankers appeared to be sailing through with transponders switched off, with an Indian-flagged supertanker and a UAE-based bulk carrier emerging in the Gulf of Oman after going dark inside the Persian Gulf.

London marine insurers reported fewer inquiries for Hormuz transits and rising cover costs. Electronic interference also resurfaced — vessels southeast of Limah in Oman appeared to travel at speeds of 30+ knots, consistent with defense systems activating to obstruct drones. The Omani corridor, established under the interim deal, showed no observable traffic.
sections.whyItMatters: The near-total halt in Hormuz traffic, just three weeks after the strait partially reopened, shows how fragile the de-escalation was. With insurers pulling back and tankers going dark, the physical disruption to global energy flows is deepening regardless of what happens diplomatically.

sections.heading: Qatar Pauses LNG Revival at Ras Laffan After Tanker Strike
sections.body:
QatarEnergy has suspended plans to rapidly restore production at the world's largest LNG facility, the Ras Laffan complex, after the attack on the Al Rekayyat tanker. CEO Saad Al-Kaabi decided to keep operations at minimum levels and reduce the number of vessels scheduled to dock, according to Bloomberg and the Express Tribune. Qatar had been preparing to revive most LNG production within two months of the peace deal, with some trains kept at reduced capacity for faster ramp-up.

About 17% of Ras Laffan's capacity was damaged by a separate missile strike weeks earlier, with repairs expected to take at least three years. The facility had been largely shut since early March after an Iranian drone attack. QatarEnergy also extended force majeure notices on LNG supplies to some Asian customers until August.

Asian LNG spot prices remain more than 80% above pre-war levels, while European benchmark gas prices rose above €50/MWh ($57.1) on Thursday for the first time since the interim deal. Pakistan is urgently seeking an LNG cargo after the Hormuz disruptions, Bloomberg reported.
sections.whyItMatters: Qatar's decision to keep Ras Laffan at minimum output — rather than risk further tanker strikes — removes the world's largest LNG supplier from the market recovery at exactly the wrong time. Asian and European gas buyers face a tighter market heading into winter, with spot prices already elevated.

sections.heading: Russia Bans Diesel Exports as Ukrainian Drones Torch Refineries
sections.body:
Russia banned diesel fuel exports on July 8, at least through the end of the month, after Ukrainian drone strikes on its refining infrastructure triggered widespread domestic fuel shortages. The ban comes as Ukraine's deep-strike campaign against Russian oil infrastructure has escalated dramatically in scale and frequency through 2026.

The Saratov Oil Refinery halted processing on July 8 after a Ukrainian drone strike hit its sole crude distillation unit (CDU-6). The facility processes roughly 147,000 barrels/day (20,000 metric tons daily) and produces over 20 types of petroleum products, including gasoline, diesel, and fuel oil. Ukraine's General Staff says the refinery helps supply the Russian military. Further strikes on July 9-10 hit oil depots in Tver (causing a long-lasting fire) and the Ilsky refinery in southern Russia, while 21 vessels were struck in Ukraine's drone offensive on Russian oil infrastructure, according to MSN and PBS.

At least 15 Russian regions have imposed gasoline sales restrictions, with rationing and price hikes spreading across the country. The Kyiv Independent reported that the Moscow refinery — Russia's largest fuel supplier — is unlikely to resume operations this year after earlier Ukrainian attacks.

UK diesel wholesale prices jumped nearly 14% on the Russian export ban, according to AOL, while European diesel refining margins have been pushed to record levels.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's diesel export ban removes a major supplier from the global middle-distillate market just as Hormuz disruptions squeeze crude supply. The convergence of both crises — one voluntary (Russia hoarding fuel), one involuntary (Hormuz at a standstill) — is driving diesel and gas prices higher worldwide.

sections.heading: Gulf Producers Launch Asian Price War as Hormuz Partially Reopens
sections.body:
Saudi Arabia is offering rare price discounts to Asian buyers to regain market share, as Gulf producers compete aggressively following the partial reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, MSN and Bloomberg reported. The kingdom's pricing move reflects a calculation that even limited transit through Hormuz is enough to restart the fight for Asian customers who diversified supply sources during the war.

The price war comes as Gulf companies brace for unequal financial tolls from the Iran conflict. Bloomberg reported that the corporate earnings season starting this week will reveal which Gulf businesses weathered the war better, with logistics, aviation, and petrochemical firms hit hardest.
sections.whyItMatters: Saudi Arabia's willingness to discount — a tool it deploys sparingly — signals that Gulf producers see the post-ceasefire market as a race to recapture displaced volumes. Asian buyers who turned to US shale, West African, and Russian crude during the Hormuz closure are now being wooed back with price incentives, but renewed attacks on shipping could nullify the strategy overnight.

sections.heading: Iraq Demands Fair OPEC Quota Ahead of White House Visit
sections.body:
Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi stated that Iraq will not leave OPEC but will continue to demand a fair production quota reflecting its capabilities and revenue needs. In an interview with Al Arabiya, Zaidi said Iraq is analyzing the implications of exceeding OPEC's ceiling, Reuters and Iraqi News reported. Last month, Reuters reported that Iraq — OPEC's second-largest producer — was considering quitting if it couldn't dramatically expand output.

The push comes as Iraq faces a serious financial crisis from the Iran war, which has disrupted its own oil exports and income. Zaidi is scheduled to visit the White House in mid-July, where he is expected to announce a political and economic partnership with the United States alongside his OPEC quota demands.

The OPEC is no longer relevant in a changing energy world, MSN reported, citing analysis that the group's influence over global oil prices has eroded as non-OPEC production grows, geopolitical disruptions reshape trade flows, and China's demand trajectory becomes uncertain.
sections.whyItMatters: Iraq's quota push is the latest sign of OPEC's internal strain. With the Iran war disrupting its own exports, Baghdad needs higher output just to stay solvent — but conceding to Iraq would mean other members giving up market share. The White House meeting adds a geopolitical dimension: US support for Iraqi production could reshape Gulf energy politics.

sections.heading: G7 Sanctions Squeeze on Russia's Shadow Fleet Intensifies
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, Canada imposed new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet, energy revenues, defense-industrial sector, and disinformation entities. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a new sanctions package targeting Russia's shadow oil fleet and finance networks, and British troops seized a Russian shadow fleet vessel in the English Channel — the first such seizure — a Cameroon-flagged tanker named Smyrtos.

The shadow fleet is adapting. Three sanctioned Russian-flagged tankers — Provider, Nasledie, and Transformer, carrying up to 2.4 million barrels of oil worth over $100 million — were tracked heading through the English Channel under Russian flag, possibly escorted by Russian warships, EADaily reported. Unlike earlier shadow fleet vessels that sail under flags of convenience, these are Russian-flagged, complicating interdiction. Other sanctioned tankers — Salut, Mystery, and Listiga — have diverted around the north of the UK to avoid the Channel.

France has taken a different approach: seizing shadow fleet tankers but releasing them after a fine of roughly $3 per barrel, effectively turning sanctions enforcement into a transit fee. The EU and UK continue to search for legal grounds to seize vessels, as sanctions alone do not provide sufficient basis under international maritime law.
sections.whyItMatters: The shadow fleet is becoming a direct confrontation zone. Russia is now escorting sanctioned tankers with naval vessels through the English Channel, while Western powers are improvising legal mechanisms to seize them. The $3/barrel French 'fine' approach reveals the gap between political sanctions and enforceable maritime law.

sections.heading: Japan's Energy-Shock Inflation Hits 3-Year High, BOJ Signals More Hikes
sections.body:
Japan's wholesale inflation surged 7.1% year-on-year in June, the fastest pace since March 2023, driven by a 22.8% rise in fuel prices and a 39.2% jump in non-ferrous metals, according to Bank of Japan data. The reading exceeded the median market forecast of 6.8% and accelerated from a revised 6.6% in May. The yen-based import price index rose 29.7%, further highlighting the cost of imported energy.

The data bolstered the case for further BOJ rate hikes, after the bank raised its policy rate to a 31-year high of 1% in June. Economists at Sompo Institute Plus warned that if wholesale inflation persists, the BOJ may be forced to raise rates as early as October. However, core consumer inflation stayed below the BOJ's 2% target for a fourth straight month, partly due to government fuel subsidies.

The BOJ's regional economic report, released Thursday, maintained its assessment for all nine regions but warned that pass-through of input costs was proceeding faster than in the past and could push consumer inflation higher later in the year.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is the clearest example of how the Iran war's energy shock is propagating through the global economy. A country dependent on imported fuel, with a weakening currency, is seeing producer prices explode — forcing its central bank into tightening even as consumer demand stays soft. The Iran-Hormuz crisis is no longer just a shipping story; it's a macroeconomic one.

bottomLine: The collapse of the Iran ceasefire into renewed strikes has sent Strait of Hormuz traffic back toward wartime lows, with Qatar pausing its LNG revival and tankers going dark to avoid attacks. Russia's diesel export ban — forced by Ukraine's devastating refinery campaign — removes another major supply source from a market already squeezed by Hormuz. Gulf producers are discounting crude to Asia in a race for market share, Iraq is pushing OPEC for higher quotas ahead of a White House visit, and the shadow fleet confrontation in the English Channel is escalating into a naval chess game. Meanwhile, Japan's 7.1% wholesale inflation shows the Iran war's energy shock is now driving macro policy in the world's fourth-largest economy. The energy system is absorbing simultaneous shocks on three fronts — Hormuz, Ukrainian drones, and sanctions enforcement — with limited buffer left.
sources: Bloomberg, Reuters, Hindu Business Line, Express Tribune, Arab Times, Kyiv Independent, PBS, MSN, EADaily, Iraqi News, Al Arabiya, Bank of Japan, Sompo Institute Plus, AOL Finance, Channel News Asia, Climate Home News, Kpler, S&P Global, Financial Express
